coaching.coaching: Pin down ADF-dict decode path within outbound comment normalization with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: ADF-dict decode path within outbound comment normalization

coaching.coaching: Plan how all consumers of the shared ADF-decode helper will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: all consumers of the shared ADF-decode helper

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for applier.py re-exports of inbound ADF symbols to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: applier.py re-exports of inbound ADF symbols

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State which port role serves the outbound-comment ADF decode at outbound_comments.py:134 (e.g. FieldSanitizer's new comment-fit surface accepts dict input, or InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text is invoked here) so _ADF_KEY deletion does not orphan _normalize_comment_body.
evidence: Plan: 'FieldSanitizer ... gains the comment-fit surface ... (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py)' and 'the two literal keys at outbound_comments.py:68,71 are deleted.'
outbound_comments.py:134 uses _load_adf().adf_to_text(body) inside _normalize_comment_body to DECODE a Jira ADF comment dict to text for diff comparison — a decode, not a send-side fit/truncate.
The plan assigns rich-text decode to InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text but only on the inbound apply path; the outbound comment-diff decode at :134 is not assigned to a port role.
findings.finding: outbound_comments.py's _ADF_KEY (line 68) also backs an ADF-DECODE call at _normalize_comment_body:134 (adf_to_text on inbound Jira comment bodies), but the plan routes the outbound comment path only through FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment (fit/truncate) and does not name which port role replaces this decode when the key is deleted.
scenarios: Outbound comment diff where jira_comments carry ADF-dict bodies (Jira Cloud v3), normalized via _normalize_comment_body before the dedup membership test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the ADF-dict→text normalization leg of `_normalize_comment_body` (outbound_comments.py:133-134, using `_ADF_KEY`) is eliminated: either (a) route it through `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` on the inbound (Jira-side) body decode — mirroring its use in inbound_translate — and document this second call site in the Approach, or (b) explicitly extend `sanitize_comment`'s type signature to `(body: Any) -> str` with that change reflected in the FieldSanitizer Protocol and the Scope section.
evidence: Plan says FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment gains 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py'
_backend.py:141: `def sanitize_comment(self, body: str) -> str` — takes str, not Any
outbound_comments.py:133-134: `if isinstance(body, dict): text = _load_adf().adf_to_text(body)` — the ADF-dict decode path uses _ADF_KEY and is separate from truncation
outbound_comments.py:324: `compare_body = _load_comment_limits().truncate_comment_body(body)` — the truncation uses _COMMENT_LIMITS_KEY separately
Plan mentions normalize_rich_text only for inbound_translate.py, not for the outbound comment diff path where Jira bodies also arrive as ADF dicts
findings.finding: The plan routes outbound_comments.py's _ADF_KEY usage through sanitizer.sanitize_comment, but sanitize_comment has signature (body: str) -> str and cannot decode ADF-dict typed Jira comment bodies — the ADF-dict→text leg of _normalize_comment_body (outbound_comments.py:133-134) is silently left unaddressed, so _ADF_KEY cannot actually be deleted.
scenarios: Implementer routes truncation through sanitize_comment but cannot route the ADF-dict → text decode through it (type mismatch), so _ADF_KEY literal remains and the sweep gate fails.
Implementer widens sanitize_comment to accept Any — but then the FieldSanitizer Protocol signature changes, breaking the port contract and requiring every backend to update its sanitizer.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the plan's Approach section to note that _normalize_comment_body's ADF-decode responsibility (for Jira comment bodies arriving as dicts) is routed through `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text(body)`, making the Jira comment bodies text before the truncation/comparison step — consistent with InboundMapper's existing role of decoding vendor-shaped payloads. This keeps sanitize_comment's signature as (body: str) -> str and naturally eliminates both _ADF_KEY and _COMMENT_LIMITS_KEY from outbound_comments.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the _normalize_adf_body consumers outside inbound_translate.py (apply_inbound_records.py:145,268,308 and applier.py:125,367) and state whether the helper is retained as an InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text delegate or those call sites are rewired.
evidence: Plan: 'inbound_translate.py calls the backend's inbound mapper; the literal key at inbound_translate.py:315 is deleted' — Scope lists only inbound_translate.py for this change.
apply_inbound_records.py:35 imports _normalize_adf_body and calls it at :145 (CREATE description), :268 (comment body), :308 (EDIT description) — all decode Jira ADF dicts to text.
applier.py:125 re-imports _normalize_adf_body and re-exports it at :367.

Deleting _ADF_KEY_APPLIER/_load_adf_module (backing _normalize_adf_body) leaves these call sites' ADF decode without a resolved implementation unless the plan states _normalize_adf_body is retained as a port delegate.
findings.finding: _normalize_adf_body — the shared ADF-decode helper the plan reroutes in inbound_translate.py — has consumers in other modules (apply_inbound_records.py, applier.py) that Scope does not enumerate.
findings.location: Approach (inbound path) / Scope; inbound_translate.py:350; apply_inbound_records.py:35,145,268,308; applier.py:125,367
scenarios: Live inbound snapshot with a raw ADF description reaches _apply_inbound_create/_inbound_update_write_edit_event.
An inbound Jira comment with an ADF body reaches the COMMENT-event writer in apply_inbound_records.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope/Approach that _normalize_adf_body is retained in inbound_translate.py delegating to the backend's normalize_rich_text (preserving the re-export applier.py and apply_inbound_records.py depend on), so only the vendor lazy-key is removed while the shared symbol's callers stay resolved.

findings.finding: `apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` directly from `inbound_translate.py` (line 35) and calls it at lines 145, 268, and 308, but `apply_inbound_records.py` is absent from the plan's Scope. When the plan replaces `_normalize_adf_body` with the port-mediated `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` call, this direct import will break.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that adding `normalize_rich_text` to `InboundMapper` is justified at this story's scope: either extend scope to route `apply_inbound_records.py` (lines 145, 268, 308) and `applier.py` through the protocol method (satisfying Rule-of-Three with ≥3 wired call sites), or keep `normalize_rich_text` as a private helper in `inbound_translate.py` and delete the single `inbound_translate.py` literal key without promoting it to a Protocol surface.
evidence: Plan: 'InboundMapper gains `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str`'
`apply_inbound_records.py:145`: `"description": _normalize_adf_body(fields.get("description"))`
`apply_inbound_records.py:268`: `body_text = _normalize_adf_body(jc.get("body"))`
`apply_inbound_records.py:308`: `"description": lambda v: _normalize_adf_body(v) if isinstance(v, dict) else v`
`applier.py:125`: `_normalize_adf_body,` (imported from inbound_translate)
Scope list does NOT include `apply_inbound_records.py` or `applier.py`
Rule-of-Three: 1 post-landing protocol call site vs. 4 remaining direct call sites
findings.finding: `normalize_rich_text` added to the `InboundMapper` Protocol will have exactly ONE post-landing call site (the `inbound_translate.py` path being rerouted), while three existing `_normalize_adf_body` call sites in `apply_inbound_records.py` (lines 145, 268, 308) and one in `applier.py` (line 125) remain out of scope and keep calling the private function directly. A new Protocol method with a single consumer fails the Rule-of-Three bar for promoting a helper to a role surface.
scenarios: A second backend author implementing `InboundMapper` must provide `normalize_rich_text`, but 4 of the 5 actual call sites of the same logic bypass the protocol entirely — the abstraction provides no uniformity guarantee.

The stated epic goal ('a second backend drops in behind the port') is only partially served: `apply_inbound_records.py` and `applier.py` still hard-wire `_normalize_adf_body` from an `inbound_translate` import that may itself contain Jira-specific logic.

findings.finding: applier.py re-imports _ADF_KEY_APPLIER and _AdfModule_Applier from inbound_translate (applier.py:110-130, listed in __all__ at applier.py:322/326), but applier.py is excluded from the plan's Scope section. Deleting _ADF_KEY_APPLIER from inbound_translate.py without updating applier.py's re-import block will cause an ImportError breaking the entire applier module.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py (remove re-imports of _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier from inbound_translate; remove from __all__)' to the Scope section, and add a corresponding testing note that the applier module imports cleanly after the change.

findings.finding: `applier.py` imports and re-exports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module`, and `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate.py` (lines 110-130, `__all__` lines 322, 326, 361, 367), but `applier.py` is absent from the plan's Scope. Deleting `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER` from `inbound_translate.py` and replacing `_normalize_adf_body` with the port-routed call will break `applier.py`'s import block and leave its `__all__` listing stale symbols.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `applier.py` to the Scope. After rerouting `inbound_translate.py`, update `applier.py`'s import block to either (a) drop `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier` from its re-export list (no external callers were found for these), or (b) replace `_normalize_adf_body` with the new port-mediated call and propagate via `__all__` accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact method name and signature FieldSanitizer gains for comment-fit (e.g. `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` absorbs truncation, or a new `fit_comment(body: str, *, limit_bytes: int) -> str` is added) so `_backend.py` Protocol and `adapters/jira/backend.py` implementor have an unambiguous contract.
evidence: 'FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface the outbound comment path actually needs (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py)'
InboundMapper's new method IS named: 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str'
ABSENCE: no method name or signature is given for FieldSanitizer's new capability
findings.finding: The plan names the new `InboundMapper` method explicitly (`normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str`) but never names the new method or signature being added to `FieldSanitizer` for 'comment-fit surface / fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation'. An executor must decide unilaterally whether to extend the existing `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` in-place or add a distinct new method (e.g. `format_comment`, `fit_comment`). The `_backend.py` Protocol definition and the Jira `adapters/jira/backend.py` implementation must agree on the signature — leaving this unnamed creates a silent split risk.
findings.location: Approach section, FieldSanitizer paragraph; Scope bullet: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py`
scenarios: Executor extends `sanitize_comment` in-place, changing its semantics in a way that breaks other callers already using it for simple validation.
Executor adds a new unnamed method, choosing an arbitrary name that conflicts with a future port-role standard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the Approach/Scope sections: 'FieldSanitizer gains `format_comment(body: str) -> str` (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation); `sanitize_comment` is unchanged. `outbound_comments.py` calls `backend.sanitizer.format_comment(body)` instead of the lazy-loaded vendor path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or justify why expand-contract is unnecessary) for the deletion of the lazy-load routing paths in outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py — e.g., note that the deletion is a single-commit revert or describe the feature-flag/phased approach if one is warranted.
evidence: 'the two literal keys at outbound_comments.py:68,71 are deleted'
'the literal key at inbound_translate.py:315 is deleted'
No section mentions a rollback strategy, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence for the routing change.
findings.finding: The plan deletes the lazy-load routing paths in outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py (an irreversible step in production) without specifying a rollback or expand-contract sequence. If the new sanitizer/mapper route has a runtime defect after deployment, there is no documented back-out path — the old literal-key paths will already be gone.
findings.location: Approach (outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py routing change); Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: The Jira backend's sanitizer implementation has a latent truncation bug not caught by unit tests; the old lazy-load path is gone and a hotfix rollback requires re-introducing the deleted code manually.

A deployment environment's registry seam behaves differently from test (e.g., config loading order), causing the sanitizer/mapper to resolve incorrectly; with no fallback path, the failure is silent until comments are malformed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to Approach: 'This change is a single atomic commit; back-out is a git revert of the commit, which restores the lazy-key literals and removes the new Protocol methods. No data migration is involved.' This satisfies T4's reversibility requirement without adding engineering overhead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in `_backend.py` docstrings or an AGENTS.md note) what non-Jira backends must return for `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment` and `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text`, and confirm that any existing test-double or stub backends either implement or inherit sensible defaults for these new members.
evidence: Plan scopes only adapters/jira/backend.py and inbound_fields.py as implementors of the new methods
Epic goal is 'a second backend drops in behind the port' — implying future non-Jira implementors exist or are imminent
No mention of stub/null/base implementations, default method bodies, or NotImplementedError stubs for the new protocol members
No ADR or AGENTS.md update is mentioned for the protocol contract change
findings.finding: The plan extends two existing Protocol roles (FieldSanitizer adds comment-fit surface; InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text) but does not specify what non-Jira backend implementations (stubs, null-adapters, or a future second backend) must provide for these new members — leaving the protocol contract change undocumented for implementors who must satisfy it.
scenarios: A developer adds a second backend and misses the new protocol members — type-checker catches it only if the Protocol is structurally enforced; runtime silently breaks if not
A stub/test-double backend used in unit tests does not implement the new methods, causing test-collection errors after the plan lands
findings.suggested_fix: Add a default/identity implementation note in _backend.py (e.g., sanitize_comment returns body unchanged; normalize_rich_text returns str(body)) so stub backends satisfy the protocol without vendor code, and add a one-liner to AGENTS.md under the port-roles section recording the new members.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or document in the plan) that `select_backend(load_config())` resolves correctly when `outbound_comments.py` is spec-loaded by file path in tests — or specify the injection mechanism (e.g., a `sanitizer` parameter) needed to preserve the existing standalone-load test model.
evidence: outbound_comments.py module docstring: 'Like outbound_differ, this module keeps its OWN lazy _load_adf / _load_comment_limits loaders (the reconciler modules are spec-loaded under test, where from . import does not resolve)'
outbound_comments.py:49-61: bootstrap of _loader via spec_from_file_location because the module is spec-loaded standalone

outbound_differ.py:450-454: the proven select_backend call is inside a function body and outbound_differ is also spec-loaded, so the two contexts are analogous — but the plan should explicitly confirm the standalone-load path for select_backend works in outbound_comments.py
Plan: 'resolves the configured backend's sanitizer via the registry seam (select_backend(load_config()) — pattern proven at outbound_differ.py:450-454)'
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `select_backend(load_config())` registry call is a 'proven' seam for `outbound_comments.py`, but that module is deliberately spec-loaded by file path in tests (its own docstring says so and it maintains private lazy-load copies for exactly this reason). The `select_backend` import requires `rebar_reconciler._backend_registry` to be importable in that standalone context — an unverified capability assertion the plan does not resolve.
findings.location: Approach section — outbound_comments.py routing; plan cites 'pattern proven at outbound_differ.py:450-454'
scenarios: A test that spec-loads outbound_comments.py standalone (as the test suite currently does for the comment-diff path) will call select_backend(load_config()) inside _diff_comments; if rebar_reconciler._backend_registry is not in sys.modules at that point, the import fails with an ImportError — breaking the existing test isolation model.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the `outbound_mapper` injection pattern from `compute_outbound_mutations`: add an optional `sanitizer: FieldSanitizer | None = None` parameter to `_diff_comments` (and `_map_comments_for_create` if needed), defaulting to `select_backend(load_config()).sanitizer` only when `None`. This is identical to the proven pattern and avoids the standalone-load risk entirely.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
